title: NG-SOC Learning Module 8 - Incident Response and Forensics (Sub Case 1a)
description: Instructor-led, cyber-range edition of NG-SOC D5.8 Learning Module 8. 30 console-based levels covering the full incident-response lifecycle across five ECSF roles (SOC Analyst, Incident Responder, CTI Analyst, Penetration Tester, IR Coordinator) plus the three Sub Case 1a practical activities, using the lm8-1a-3 sandbox (Wazuh SIEM, vulnerable banking app, C2 simulation, ransomware artefacts, DFIR toolset, MISP, Kali). Aligned to NIST SP 800-61r2, ENISA ECSF and MITRE ATT&CK.
prerequisites: Basic Linux command line (ssh, ls, cat, grep, jq, curl).
Familiarity with the incident-response lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61r2).
Awareness of SIEM alert triage and basic networking (IP, ports, DNS).
outcomes: Apply incident-response procedures across all IR lifecycle phases.
Perform SIEM-based alert analysis and triage (Wazuh) to separate true threats from noise.
Use threat intelligence (MISP/IoCs) to inform proactive defence.
Conduct forensic analysis and malware/ransomware impact assessment with DFIR tooling.
Simulate adversary behaviour ethically against a banking app and report findings (ATT&CK).
Coordinate and evaluate an IR plan via a ransomware tabletop and produce an After-Action Report.

levels.title: Module 8 - Overview & House Rules
levels.content:
# NG-SOC Learning Module 8 - Incident Response and Forensics (Sub Case 1a)

Welcome to the instructor-led, cyber-range edition of **Learning Module 8**. Over **39 levels** you will work the full incident-response lifecycle (NIST SP 800-61r2: *Preparation -> Detection & Analysis -> Containment, Eradication & Recovery -> Post-Incident*) across the five ECSF-aligned roles of the module:

| Exercise | Role (ECSF) | Levels |
|---|---|---|
| Practical 2.0.1 | Multi-channel spear-phishing | 9-10 |
| Practical 2.0.2 | Collaborative response chain | 11-12 |
| Practical 2.0.3 | Express forensic report | 20-24 |
| **Ex. 1** | SOC Analyst - Triage | 2-8 |
| **Ex. 2** | Incident Responder - Containment/Eradication | 13-19 |
| **Ex. 3** | CTI Analyst - *Black Falcon* briefing | 25-29 |
| **Ex. 4** | Penetration Tester - banking system | 30-33 |
| **Ex. 5** | IR Coordinator - ransomware tabletop | 34-37 |

**The scenario.** A financial institution running an online-banking platform is targeted by the fictitious **"Black Falcon"** campaign: spear-phishing -> trojan on a workstation -> C2 beacon -> lateral movement -> ransomware on the file server. Every artefact in this range is **synthetic and contained**.

**How answers work.** Most levels are *console* tasks: you log into a sandbox host and use the tools provisioned by the range (Wazuh, `jq`, `grep`, `nmap`, `curl`, The Sleuth Kit, `strings`, MISP, Kali) to recover a **flag** - a real value present on the machines. Submit it exactly as shown.

**Reference frameworks** used throughout: NIST SP 800-61r2, ENISA ECSF, MITRE ATT&CK, FIRST CSIRT Services Framework.

> Tip: use the **Chrome** browser. If a submission misbehaves, log out and back in.

levels.title: Access the Range
levels.cloud_content:
Before any analysis you must reach the range. The topology appears on the right.

### Connect
1. In the topology, right-click a host (start with **`analyst-host`** or **`siem`**) -> **Open console**.
2. Log in as **`analyst`** (the student account provisioned on every node). For multi-hop access to the internal segments, use **`proxy-jump`** as the SSH jump host.
3. Useful segments: **DMZ** `10.10.20.0/24`, **Corporate** `10.10.30.0/24`, **Security-tools** `10.10.40.0/24`, **Red-team** `10.10.50.0/24`.

When connected, submit the passkey **`ngsoc-start`** to begin.

SSH access (easier copy-paste) is described in the [sandbox-access docs](https://docs.platform.cyberrange.cz/user-guide-advanced/sandboxes/sandbox-access/).
levels.local_content: This training is designed for the cloud sandbox. Local-sandbox access has not been validated for this topology.

levels.title: Exercise 1 - Suspicious Network Activity (SOC Analyst Triage)
levels.content:
## Scenario (Detection & Analysis)
Night shift at the SOC. The **SIEM (`siem`, 10.10.40.10, Wazuh manager)** alerts on unusual outbound traffic from an employee workstation toward an IP **known for hosting banking malware**, and the endpoint agent logs a *"possible Trojan infection."* No user has reported anything yet.

**Your role - SOC Analyst (Tier 1).** Triage the alert: decide real incident vs. false positive, capture IoCs, take proportionate first containment, and prepare a clean hand-over.

The pre-seeded detection content lives on `siem`:
- Custom rules: `/var/ossec/etc/rules/ngsoc_lm8_rules.xml`
- Pre-seeded alert bundle: `/opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json`

Levels 3-7 walk the **observables**; level 8 assesses your **triage decision**.

levels.title: Alert Analysis - the C2 IP address
levels.content:
Open the pre-seeded alert bundle on **`siem`** and read the highest-severity alert (Wazuh **rule 100001**, level 13: *outbound connection to a known Black Falcon C2 IP*).

**Flag:** the destination C2 **IPv4 address** the workstation is beaconing to.
levels.solution:
1. On `siem`: `sudo cat /opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json | jq '.[]? // .'` - or simply `grep -o '"dstip":"[^"]*"' /opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json`.
2. Cross-check the rule text: `grep -A2 'rule id="100001"' /var/ossec/etc/rules/ngsoc_lm8_rules.xml`.
3. The destination is **`${ANSWER}`** (host `c2-server`).

hints.title: Where to look
hints.content: The file `/opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json` on `siem` contains the alert in JSON. `jq` and `grep` are installed.

hints.title: Field name
hints.content: Look for the `dstip` field, or read the description of Wazuh rule 100001.

levels.title: Alert Analysis - the C2 port
levels.content:
Same alert. The beacon is not using a normal web port - that is part of why it is suspicious.

**Flag:** the destination **TCP port** of the C2 channel.
levels.solution:
1. `grep -o '"dstport":"[^"]*"' /opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json` on `siem`.
2. Rule 100001 names it explicitly: `10.10.20.20:${ANSWER}`.
3. Port **`${ANSWER}`** is a classic non-standard C2 listener port (Metasploit default).

hints.title: Non-standard port
hints.content: Web traffic is 80/443. The alert flags a *different* port - read `dstport` in the JSON.

levels.title: Alert Analysis - the C2 domain (DNS IoC)
levels.content:
A separate, lower-severity alert (Wazuh **rule 100005**) fired for a **DNS query to the campaign's C2 domain**. The same name is visible in the syslog the workstation generated (tag `svc_update` on **`employee-ws`**).

**Flag:** the fully-qualified **C2 domain name**.
levels.solution:
1. On `siem`: `grep dns_query /opt/ngsoc-alerts/ex1-preseeded-alerts.json`.
2. Or on `employee-ws`: `grep svc_update /var/log/syslog` and read the *suspicious DNS query* line.
3. The C2 domain is **`${ANSWER}`** (it resolves to `10.10.20.20` in `/etc/hosts`).

hints.title: Two places to look
hints.content: Either the pre-seeded JSON on `siem` (`dns_query`) or the syslog on `employee-ws` (`grep svc_update /var/log/syslog`).

levels.title: Endpoint Analysis - the malware process stub
levels.content:
Move to the affected host **`employee-ws`** (10.10.30.10). The endpoint agent flagged a *possible Trojan*. A planted **executable stub** is masquerading as a service updater.

**Flag:** the **absolute path** of the malicious stub file.
levels.solution:
1. On `employee-ws`: `ls -la /tmp/ | grep -i svc` (note the leading dot - it is hidden).
2. Confirm it is referenced by the alert (`process` field) and by the beacon.
3. The stub is **`${ANSWER}`** (MITRE T1543 - persistent service stub).

hints.title: Hidden file
hints.content: It lives in `/tmp` and the name starts with a dot, so use `ls -la /tmp`.

levels.title: Endpoint Analysis - the persistence mechanism
levels.content:
Still on `employee-ws`. The malware keeps itself alive on a schedule (MITRE **T1053.005 - Scheduled Task/Job: Cron**).

**Flag:** the **absolute path** of the cron file that re-launches the beacon.
levels.solution:
1. `ls -la /etc/cron.d/` and inspect entries: `cat /etc/cron.d/svc-update`.
2. It periodically runs `/opt/c2-beacon/beacon.sh`.
3. The persistence file is **`${ANSWER}`**.

hints.title: Where cron jobs live
hints.content: System cron fragments are in `/etc/cron.d/`. List that directory.

levels.title: Exercise 1 - Triage Decision (Assessment)

questions.text: Which phase of the NIST SP 800-61r2 incident-response lifecycle are you in for this event?
Given a confirmed C2 beacon plus an endpoint Trojan alert on a corporate host, the correct triage action is to:

choices.text: Escalate to the CSIRT / on-call Incident Responder and begin containment
Wait for a user to report a problem

questions.text: Which Wazuh rule ID fired for the outbound C2 connection? (number only)
A proportionate *immediate* containment step during analysis would be to:

choices.text: Power off the entire corporate segment
Network-isolate the affected workstation and block the C2 IP/port at the perimeter
Pay attention but take no action

levels.instructions: Answer as the on-duty SOC analyst writing the initial incident record. Use what you found in levels 3-7.

levels.title: Practical 2.0.1 - Multi-channel Spear-Phishing Simulation
levels.content:
## Purpose
The initial access for *Black Falcon* is **spear-phishing across channels** (email, SMS, voice-call script). The red-team artefacts are staged on **`kali`** (10.10.50.10); the **triage note template** you must fill is staged with the student hand-outs on **`db-server`** (`/home/analyst/handouts/`).

**Assessed learner outputs:** a phishing **triage note** (lure theme, intent, immediate risk), a list of **observables** (sender domains, URLs, attachment hashes), and a **recommended response action set**.

Level 10 confirms you can locate the triage-note template you will complete and hand over.

levels.title: Phishing triage - locate the triage-note template
levels.content:
On **`db-server`**, the instructor staged the analyst hand-out templates under `/home/analyst/handouts/`. Find the one used to record a phishing triage.

**Flag:** the **filename** (just the name, e.g. `something.md`) of the phishing triage-note template.
levels.solution:
1. On `db-server`: `ls /home/analyst/handouts/`.
2. The phishing triage template is **`${ANSWER}`**; open it with `cat` to see the required fields (lure theme, observables, response actions).

hints.title: Hand-out directory
hints.content: Templates are under `/home/analyst/handouts/` on `db-server`. List it.

levels.title: Practical 2.0.2 - Collaborative Response Chain
levels.content:
## Purpose
Incident handling as a coordinated team using REP collaboration features: a **task board**, a **decision log**, a **SITREP** for management, and **hand-over notes**. The corresponding templates are staged on **`db-server`** under `/home/analyst/handouts/`.

**Assessed outputs:** maintained task/action list, decision log with rationale, concise SITREP, clear hand-over notes - consistent with each other.

levels.title: Collaboration - locate the SITREP template
levels.content:
Still on **`db-server`** (`/home/analyst/handouts/`), identify the **situation-report** template the team uses to brief management.

**Flag:** the **filename** of the SITREP template.
levels.solution:
1. On `db-server`: `ls /home/analyst/handouts/` - you will also see `decision-log.md` and `handover-notes.md`.
2. The management situation report is **`${ANSWER}`**.

hints.title: Naming
hints.content: SITREP = situation report. Look for the template whose name contains `sitrep`.

levels.title: Exercise 2 - Containment & Eradication of a Targeted Attack (Incident Responder)
levels.content:
## Scenario (Containment, Eradication & Recovery)
The SOC has **confirmed** a targeted intrusion: a web-server vulnerability on **`web-banking`** (10.10.20.10) was exploited, malware spread, and **critical customer data may have been exfiltrated**. The CSIRT is engaged and the IR plan is active. Persistence and covert channels are in play.

**Your role - Incident Responder (CSIRT Analyst).** Scope the compromise, contain, eradicate, and investigate - preserving evidence (write-blockers, hashing, chain-of-custody). This exercise also delivers the **Express Forensic Report (Practical 2.0.3)**: use the DFIR tools on **`analyst-host`** (The Sleuth Kit, `strings`/binutils, `binwalk`, `foremost`, `hashdeep`, Volatility 3) to build a timeline from the artefacts.

Levels 14-18 are the technical investigation; level 19 assesses eradication & forensic fundamentals.

levels.title: Incident Scoping - the information-disclosure artefact
levels.content:
Exploitation of `web-banking` left a classic **information-disclosure** finding: a world-readable *backup* file under the app's public `/backup` directory (`/var/www/html/banking/backup/`). An attacker (or you) can fetch it over HTTP.

**Flag:** the **filename** of the synthetic backup file exposed in `/backup`.
levels.solution:
1. From `kali` or `analyst-host`: `curl -s http://10.10.20.10/backup/` (directory listing) - or on `web-banking`: `ls /var/www/html/banking/backup/`.
2. The exposed artefact is **`${ANSWER}`** (MITRE T1083 - File and Directory Discovery).

hints.title: Two ways
hints.content: Over HTTP with `curl http://10.10.20.10/backup/`, or locally `ls /var/www/html/banking/backup/` on `web-banking`.

levels.title: Forensics - identify patient zero
levels.content:
Reconstruct the intrusion path. The **first** compromised internal host (*patient zero*) is the workstation that received the phishing payload and beaconed to C2.

**Flag:** the **IPv4 address** of patient zero.
levels.solution:
1. The C2 alerts (Ex.1) and the planted stub/cron all point at `employee-ws`.
2. Confirm its address: on `employee-ws` run `ip -4 addr` or check `/etc/hosts`.
3. Patient zero is **`${ANSWER}`** (`employee-ws`, corporate segment).

hints.title: Trace it back
hints.content: Patient zero is the host with the Trojan stub and the C2 beacon from Exercise 1.

levels.title: Forensics - recover the ransomware key ID
levels.content:
The intrusion ended in **ransomware** on **`file-server`** (10.10.30.20). Read the ransom note in the share (`/srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt`) and extract the campaign's tracking identifier.

**Flag:** the **decryption Key ID** quoted in the note.
levels.solution:
1. On `file-server`: `grep -i 'Key ID' /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt` (also present in each `.locked` file header).
2. The key ID is **`${ANSWER}`** (MITRE T1486 - Data Encrypted for Impact).

hints.title: Grep the note
hints.content: `grep -i 'key id' /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt` on `file-server`.

levels.title: Impact - count the encrypted files
levels.content:
Quantify the impact on `file-server`. The ransomware renamed business documents with a **`.locked`** extension in the data share `/srv/data`.

**Flag:** the **number** of `.locked` files in `/srv/data` (digits only).
levels.solution:
1. On `file-server`: `ls /srv/data/*.locked | wc -l`.
2. There are **`${ANSWER}`** simulated-encrypted files (reports, payroll, exports, board deck, IR plan, etc.).

hints.title: Count with the shell
hints.content: `ls /srv/data/*.locked | wc -l`.

levels.title: Eradication - find the beacon script to remove
levels.content:
Eradication means removing **every** foothold. Besides the cron job (Ex.1), the actual beacon logic the cron calls must be deleted from `employee-ws`.

**Flag:** the **absolute path** of the beacon script.
levels.solution:
1. On `employee-ws`: read the cron file `cat /etc/cron.d/svc-update` - it invokes the script.
2. Or `ls -R /opt/c2-beacon/`.
3. The beacon to remove is **`${ANSWER}`** (delete the script, the cron, and the `/tmp/.svc_update` stub; then block C2 at the perimeter).

hints.title: Follow the cron
hints.content: The cron fragment from Exercise 1 names the script it runs - read it.

levels.title: Exercise 2 - Eradication & Forensic Fundamentals (Assessment)

questions.text: To preserve evidence integrity when imaging a disk you should:

choices.text: Mount it read-write and copy files
Use a write-blocker and record cryptographic hashes (chain-of-custody)

questions.text: Which MITRE ATT&CK technique ID describes the ransomware impact (data encrypted for impact)?
Which MITRE ATT&CK technique ID describes exploiting the public-facing banking web app?
Before declaring recovery complete, you should:

choices.text: Restore from clean backups, patch the exploited flaw on ALL servers, rotate credentials and verify no malicious outbound traffic
Bring systems straight back online to reduce downtime
Only clean the first host found

levels.instructions: Express-forensic-report fundamentals (Practical 2.0.3) and eradication/recovery good practice.

levels.title: Practical 2.0.3 - Express Forensic Report
levels.content:
# Practical 2.0.3 - Express Forensic Report

D5.8 Section 2.0.3 defines an **express forensic report** built under time pressure from a curated artefact bundle. The bundle is deployed on **`analyst-host`** at `/opt/ngsoc-artifacts/forensic-bundle/`:

| Artefact | Use |
|---|---|
| `wazuh-alerts-ex2.json` | SIEM alert stream (C2, lateral movement, brute-force, encryption) |
| `netstat-employee-ws.txt` | live connections on patient zero |
| `endpoint-process-list.txt` | `ps aux` snapshot |
| `file-metadata.txt` | dropper hashes/timestamps |
| `timeline-clues.txt` | partial timeline to complete |

Work the next three flags from this bundle, then complete the assessment. Map every claim to the 7-heading express-report template at `~/reports/express-forensic-report.md` (Scope, Artefacts, Timeline, Findings, Indicators, Impact, Recommendations).

levels.title: Forensics - lateral-movement destination host
levels.content:
In `wazuh-alerts-ex2.json` the attacker pivots from patient zero (`employee-ws`) over SMB to a second corporate host.

**Flag:** the **destination IPv4** of the lateral-movement (SMB/ADMIN$) alert.
levels.solution:
1. On `analyst-host`: `grep -i lateral /opt/ngsoc-artifacts/forensic-bundle/wazuh-alerts-ex2.json` (rule 100003).
2. Read the `dstip` field.
3. The pivot target is **`${ANSWER}`** (`file-server`).

hints.title: Which rule?
hints.content: Wazuh rule 100003 describes an SMB/PsExec lateral-movement pattern.

hints.title: Field
hints.content: Look at the `dstip` of that alert, not the `srcip` (which is patient zero).

levels.title: Forensics - the brute-forced account on db-server
levels.content:
The alert stream shows repeated failed SSH logins to `db-server` followed by a successful one (rule 5715, *successful login after repeated failures*).

**Flag:** the **username** whose login finally succeeded after the brute-force.
levels.solution:
1. On `analyst-host`: `grep 5715 /opt/ngsoc-artifacts/forensic-bundle/wazuh-alerts-ex2.json`.
2. Read the `user` field on the success event (after 10 prior failures in 2 min).
3. The compromised account is **`${ANSWER}`**.

hints.title: Which rule?
hints.content: Rule 5715 = successful SSH login after repeated failures. Rule 5710 = the failures.

hints.title: Account
hints.content: Two accounts were targeted (`root`, then another); only the second one succeeds.

levels.title: Forensics - the dropper SHA-256 (MISP IoC)
levels.content:
`file-metadata.txt` documents the primary dropper `/tmp/.svc_update`. Its SHA-256 is the IoC you would push to the MISP Black Falcon event for hunting across the estate.

**Flag:** the **first 12 hex characters** of the dropper's SHA-256.
levels.solution:
1. On `analyst-host`: `grep -A12 "PRIMARY DROPPER" /opt/ngsoc-artifacts/forensic-bundle/file-metadata.txt`.
2. Read the full SHA256 (it matches the Black Falcon IoC in MISP) and submit its first 12 hex characters.
3. The flag is **`deadbeef0011`** (first 12 hex chars of the full SHA256).

hints.title: Where
hints.content: Open `file-metadata.txt`, ITEM 1 (PRIMARY DROPPER).

hints.title: Field
hints.content: Use the `SHA256` line (not `MD5`); submit only its first 12 hex characters.

levels.title: Practical 2.0.3 - Express Forensic Report (Assessment)

questions.text: From the bundle, the most-supported **initial access** vector is:

choices.text: Spear-phishing email -> malicious macro -> dropper in /tmp
SQL injection on the banking app
Physical access to the data centre

questions.text: The `.locked` mass-rename on `file-server` (rule 100004) maps to which ATT&CK technique?
How many files were renamed to `.locked` in the rapid-succession alert? (number only)
Given a dropper that re-establishes a second C2 channel from a child process, the priority containment action is:

choices.text: Network-isolate patient zero and block egress to the C2 IP/port
Reboot the host and hope it clears

levels.instructions: Submit as the analyst compiling the express forensic report. Base every answer on the bundle artefacts (Section 2.0.3 checklist: evidence-based, traceable, prioritised).

levels.title: Exercise 3 - Threat Intelligence Briefing (CTI Analyst)
levels.content:
## Scenario (Preparation / proactive defence)
A CTI feed warns of a new campaign targeting financial institutions, delivering malware that **hijacks banking transactions** via spear-phishing. Your **`cti`** host (10.10.40.30) runs **MISP** for IoC management.

**Your role - CTI Analyst.** Turn the *Black Falcon* intelligence into actionable guidance: threat overview & TTPs, prioritised IoCs, risk assessment, per-team recommendations, dissemination, and a concise **Threat Intelligence Brief** with an *Analyst's Notes* section.

Levels 26-28 recover the campaign identifiers/IoCs; level 29 assesses your CTI reasoning.

levels.title: CTI - name the campaign
levels.content:
Across the range, the adversary's tooling and notes carry a campaign name. Read the ransom note on `file-server` or the Wazuh rule descriptions on `siem`.

**Flag:** the **campaign name** (two words, as written, e.g. `Xxxxx Yyyyy`).
levels.solution:
1. `grep -i 'Black Falcon' /var/ossec/etc/rules/ngsoc_lm8_rules.xml` on `siem` (all rules reference the campaign name with a space).
2. Or: `grep -i 'Black Falcon' /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt` on `file-server` (header line).
3. The campaign is **`${ANSWER}`**.

hints.title: Grep for it
hints.content: `grep -i 'Black Falcon'` the Wazuh rules on `siem` or the ransom note on `file-server`.

levels.title: CTI - recover the campaign code
levels.content:
The ransom note header carries a short **campaign code** (a tracking tag of the form `BF-Qx-YYYY`).

**Flag:** the **campaign code** exactly as written.
levels.solution:
1. On `file-server`: `grep -i campaign /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt`.
2. The code is **`${ANSWER}`** - useful as a MISP event tag/correlation key.

hints.title: Header line
hints.content: It is on the *Campaign* line near the top of `README-RANSOM.txt`.

levels.title: CTI - extract the ransom wallet IoC
levels.content:
A financial IoC worth circulating to the sector ISAC: the **Bitcoin wallet** the note demands payment to.

**Flag:** the **wallet string** exactly as written in the note.
levels.solution:
1. On `file-server`: `grep -i wallet /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt`.
2. The (synthetic) wallet is **`${ANSWER}`**. In MISP you would add it as a `btc` attribute on the `Black Falcon` event and share it with the financial-sector ISAC.

hints.title: Find the wallet line
hints.content: `grep -i wallet /srv/data/README-RANSOM.txt` on `file-server`.

levels.title: Exercise 3 - CTI Reasoning (Assessment)

questions.text: Which MITRE ATT&CK technique ID is the campaign's initial access (spear-phishing)?
Which IoC is generally the MOST durable to deploy in detections (lowest false-positive churn)?

choices.text: A specific malware file hash / C2 domain tied to the campaign
The word 'invoice' in any email subject

questions.text: The proactive value of feeding this CTI into the SOC/SIEM is primarily to:

choices.text: Shorten detection time by hunting the known IoCs before impact

questions.text: Appropriate external dissemination of confirmed IoCs is:

choices.text: Post them publicly with victim names
Share via a financial-sector ISAC / national CERT under agreed handling (e.g. TLP)

levels.instructions: Threat-intelligence interpretation and prioritisation for the Black Falcon brief.

levels.title: Exercise 4 - Proactive Security Testing (Penetration Tester)
levels.content:
## Scenario (red-team / cyber range)
The bank wants its online-banking app proactively tested. From **`kali`** (10.10.50.10) you attack the deliberately vulnerable **`web-banking`** app (10.10.20.10). Stay safe and controlled; report every weakness with impact and a fix.

**Your role - Penetration Tester.** Plan the kill chain, run threat-informed testing (Nmap, curl, manual SQLi/credential testing), find at least two significant findings, map them to ATT&CK, and recommend mitigations.

Levels 31-32 are hands-on findings; level 33 assesses your reporting and mitigations.

levels.title: Pen test - weak admin credential
levels.content:
The banking app seeds an **admin account with a weak password** in the `admin_users` table of the `bankdb` database (recoverable via the login SQL-injection, or by reading the DB on `web-banking`). 

**Flag:** the **plaintext password** of the `admin` account.
levels.solution:
1. Recon first: from `kali`, `nmap -sV 10.10.20.10` (HTTP/80) and browse `http://10.10.20.10/`.
2. The app is vulnerable to SQLi on `login.php`; alternatively, on `web-banking`: `mysql -uroot -p'R00tP@ssNGSOC!' bankdb -e 'SELECT username,password,role FROM admin_users;'`.
3. The admin password is **`${ANSWER}`** (MITRE T1110 - weak/guessable credential).

hints.title: Recon then read the table
hints.content: `nmap -sV 10.10.20.10`, then query `admin_users` in `bankdb` (creds are seeded by the app).

hints.title: Direct DB read
hints.content: On `web-banking`: `mysql -uroot -p'R00tP@ssNGSOC!' bankdb -e 'select * from admin_users;'`.

levels.title: Pen test - the exposed directory
levels.content:
During web testing you confirm the **information-disclosure** finding: a publicly browsable directory under `/` that should never be exposed.

**Flag:** the **directory name** (single word) that is publicly accessible without authentication.
levels.solution:
1. From `kali`: `curl -s http://10.10.20.10/backup/` returns a listing.
2. The exposed directory is **`/${ANSWER}/`** - it leaks the synthetic DB backup from Exercise 2. Recommend removing it and adding a deny rule / WAF.

hints.title: Web enumeration
hints.content: Try common paths under `http://10.10.20.10/`; the one that lists files is the finding. You already saw it in Exercise 2.

levels.title: Exercise 4 - Findings, ATT&CK Mapping & Mitigations (Assessment)

questions.text: The SQL injection in the banking login maps to which ATT&CK technique?
The most effective code-level fix for the SQL injection is:

choices.text: Parameterised queries / prepared statements with input validation (plus a WAF rule as defence-in-depth)

questions.text: For the weak `admin` credential, the best mitigations are:

choices.text: Enforce a strong password policy, add MFA, and alert on repeated failed admin logins

questions.text: The exposed `/backup/` directory is which ATT&CK technique ID (file & directory discovery)?

levels.instructions: Map findings to ATT&CK and propose proportionate, specific mitigations for the pen-test report.

levels.title: Exercise 5 - IR Plan Review & Ransomware Tabletop (IR Coordinator)
levels.content:
# Exercise 5 - IR Plan Review & Ransomware Tabletop

**Role (ECSF):** Incident Response Coordinator / Facilitator.

D5.8 Section 2.5 runs a **tabletop** to validate the IR plan against a ransomware crisis. The scenario brief **"Operation Locked Ledger"** is deployed on **`analyst-host`** at `/opt/ngsoc-artifacts/ex5-ransomware/tabletop-scenario.txt` (four timed injects: initial assessment -> scope expands -> management decision -> recovery).

Locate and read the brief (next two flags), then complete the decision-focused assessment. This exercise is **conceptual**: it tests IR-plan decisions (authority, ransom policy, clean-backup recovery, lessons learned), not shell commands.

levels.title: Tabletop - locate the scenario brief
levels.content:
The facilitator releases the ransomware tabletop brief within REP. It is staged on `analyst-host`.

**Flag:** the **filename** of the tabletop scenario brief.
levels.solution:
1. On `analyst-host`: `ls /opt/ngsoc-artifacts/ex5-ransomware/`.
2. The brief is **`${ANSWER}`**; open it with `cat` to read the four injects.

hints.title: Where
hints.content: Look under `/opt/ngsoc-artifacts/ex5-ransomware/` on `analyst-host`.

levels.title: Tabletop - the scenario codename
levels.content:
Every tabletop has a codename used to tag the exercise after-action report.

**Flag:** the **scenario codename** (the `SCENARIO TITLE` in the brief).
levels.solution:
1. On `analyst-host`: `grep -i 'SCENARIO TITLE' /opt/ngsoc-artifacts/ex5-ransomware/tabletop-scenario.txt`.
2. The codename is **`${ANSWER}`** (Nordik Bank, 12 BTC / 48 h).

hints.title: Where
hints.content: Read the header of `tabletop-scenario.txt` (`SCENARIO TITLE:` line).

levels.title: Exercise 5 - Tabletop Decisions & Plan Gaps (Assessment)

questions.text: Inject 4: a restored server (FS-03) is **re-encrypted 4 hours after restoration**. The correct recovery action is to:

choices.text: Pay the ransom to get a working decryptor
Rebuild from a CLEAN baseline and rotate/reset all credentials before reconnecting
Restore the same backup again to the same server
Disable logging to speed up recovery

questions.text: Who holds authority to declare the incident and activate the IR plan / approve network shutdown?

choices.text: The Incident Commander (escalating to CISO/management for strategic calls)

questions.text: On the ransom-payment decision (Inject 3), the **strongest** non-financial blocker is:

choices.text: The decryptor only works ~65% of the time
The ransomware group may be on an EU sanctions list (paying may be illegal)

questions.text: What is the age (in days) of the last known-good backup in the brief? (number only)

levels.instructions: Answer as the Incident Commander facilitating "Operation Locked Ledger". Decisions should reflect IR best practice (NIST SP 800-61r2) and the injects in the brief.

levels.title: Module 8 - Wrap-up & Debrief
levels.content:
# Wrap-up

You have completed the five ECSF-aligned exercises and the three assessed Sub Case 1a practicals (2.0.1 spear-phishing, 2.0.2 collaborative response chain, 2.0.3 express forensic report) of **Learning Module 8**.

In the instructor-led debrief, the facilitator reviews each write-up, consolidates the express forensic report and the tabletop after-action report, and updates the IR plan with the gaps surfaced here. Reference frameworks: **NIST SP 800-61r2**, **ENISA ECSF**, **MITRE ATT&CK**, **FIRST CSIRT Services Framework**.
